XXXII

Cope Takes His Degree

LemoyneтАЩs departure but a fortnight before CopeтАЩs small share in the convocation seemed to hint at mutual dissatisfaction; it might even stand for a disagreement, or possibly a quarrel. тАЬItтАЩs just as well that he went,тАЭ said Randolph to himself. тАЬHis presence here was no advantage to BertramтБатАФnor to anybody else.тАЭ And with another fortnight Cope himself would be gone; and who knew in what distant quarter he might take up his autumn work? His ambitions, as Randolph knew, pointed to some important university in the East. Meanwhile, make the most of the flying days.

Medora Phillips took the same view. She let Carolyn Thorpe loose for a weekтАЩs spring vacation, and sent Cope word that she was alone in a darkened, depopulated home. Amy married. Hortense banished. Carolyn waved aside. With all such varying devotions removed, why should he not look in on her loneliness, during these final days, for dinner or tea? He was still тАЬcharmingтАЭтБатАФhowever difficult, however recalcitrant. And he was soon to depart. And who could believe that the fall term would bring his equal or his like?

Randolph, still taking his business easily, had suggestions for walks and lunches; he had also free time to make his suggestions operative. But Cope, though frequently seen in active movement on the campus and through the town, gave little heed to either of his elderly friends. He met them both, in High Street, on different occasions, and thanked and smiled and promisedтБатАФand kept away. He was doubtless absorbed in his special work, in the details of the closing year. He may have thought (as young men have been known to think) that, in accepting their invitations, he had done enough for them already. He had shown his good will on several occasions; let that suffice. Or he may have thought (as young men have been found capable of thinking) not at all: other concerns, more pressing and more contemporaneous, may have crowded them out of his mind altogether.

тАЬI wonder if itтАЩs sensitiveness?тАЭ asked Randolph of Foster. тАЬHis chum didnтАЩt go away in the best of good odor.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬSettle it for yourself,тАЭ returned Foster brusquely. тАЬAnd recall that you have an officeтБатАФand might have office-hours. Still, if you insist on asking meтБатАФтАЭ

тАЬI donтАЩt. But you may speak, if you like.тАЭ

тАЬAnd if you will consent to be fobbed off with a short-measure answerтБатАФтАЭ

тАЬThatтАЩs right. DonтАЩt say all you think.тАЭ

тАЬThen I would put it somewhere between indifference and ingratitude. Nearer the latter. We know the young.тАЭ

тАЬI donтАЩt feel that IтАЩve done so very much for him,тАЭ said Randolph, rather colorlessly.

тАЬYou were inclined to.тАЭ

тАЬHтАЩm, yes. I could have opened up avenues that would have made his year here a very different thing. Perhaps he didnтАЩt realize what I could do. And perhaps he found me too old.тАЭ

тАЬShall you attend the convocation?тАЭ

тАЬI go usually. IтАЩll push him off from shore and waft him goodbye.тАЭ

тАЬGoodbye? Good riddance!тАЭ

тАЬYou never liked him.тАЭ

тАЬI never did. If he leaves town without showing up here, no loss.тАЭ

тАЬMedora expects him here?тАЭ

тАЬI think so.тАЭ

Randolph descended to the lower floor. Mrs.┬аPhillips was alone, seated behind a tea-service that steamed with expectation.

тАЬGoing?тАЭ she asked.

тАЬGoing. Joe is grouchy and violent today. And he keeps on reminding me that I have an office.тАЭ

Medora glanced at the clock. Expectation seemed to be simmering down.

тАЬStay a few moments if you like. Forget the office a little longer. IтАЩll make some fresh.тАЭ

тАЬNot all these preparations for me?тАЭ

тАЬWell, theyтАЩre here. Take advantage.тАЭ

тАЬYouтАЩre all alone?тАЭ

тАЬAlone. The house is empty.тАЭ

Medora tried to look as if at the heart of a tremendous vacuum.

тАЬI canтАЩt fill it.тАЭ

тАЬYou can fill fifteen minutes.тАЭ

тАЬOh, if youтАЩre going to confound time and spaceтБатАКтБатАжтАК!тАЭ

He sat down receptively.

Medora rang a bell and harried Helga a little.

She glanced at Randolph. He sat there as if less to fill than to be filled.

тАЬSay something,тАЭ she said.

тАЬAre you going to the convocation?тАЭ

тАЬNo.тАЭ

He sat silent.

тАЬDoes that exhaust the subjects of interest?тАЭ she asked.

тАЬPretty nearly. DoesnтАЩt it?тАЭ

Medora fell silent in turnтБатАФlet the light clatter of the tea things speak for her.

тАЬAre you going to the convocation?тАЭ he presently asked again.

тАЬSuch variety!тАЭ she mocked.

тАЬAre you?тАЭ

She hesitated.

тАЬYes,тАЭ she said.

тАЬThatтАЩs better. LetтАЩs go togetherтБатАФas friends.тАЭ

тАЬWho would imagine us going as enemies?тАЭ

тАЬWho, indeed?тАЭ Yet if they went together they went as reconciled competitorsтБатАФthey went as the result of a truce.

тАЬI should like to see Bertram Cope in cap and gown,тАЭ he said.

тАЬHe has worn them before, he tells me.тАЭ

тАЬAs aтБатАФ?тАЭ

тАЬAs a member of the choir, during his undergraduate days.тАЭ

тАЬI see.тАЭ

тАЬI never noticed him especially, then,тАЭ she acknowledged.

тАЬWe can notice him now.тАЭ

Medora made a slight grimace. тАЬYes, we can notice.тАЭ He the actor; they the audience. тАЬA farewell performance.тАЭ

тАЬA final view.тАЭ

Convocation day came clear, fair, mild. The professors walked in colorful solemnity beneath the elms and up the middle aisle of the chapel, lending both to outdoors and indoors the enlivenment of hoods red, yellow, purple. The marshals led strings of candidatesтБатАФlong strings and shortтБатАФto the platform where the president sat, and the deans presented in due order their bachelors, masters and doctors. The rapid handing out of the diplomas brought frequent applauseтБатАФbits, spatters, volleys, as the case might be. There was recognition for a Chinaman, for a negro law-student, for a pair of Filipinos; there was a marked outburst for a husky young man who was assumed by the uninformed to have been a star in the universityтАЩs athletic life; there was a respectful but emphatic acknowledgment for a determined-looking middle-aged woman with gray hair, who was led on with four men as a little string of five; there was a salvo for a thoughtful, dignified man of thirty-odd, who went up as a group in himself, attended by marshals before and behind; and there was a slight spatter of applause for Bertram Cope (one of a small procession of six), yet rather more for a smiling young man who followed him.тБатАКтБатАж

Cope looked somewhat spare, despite his voluminous gown. The trying lights added little color to his face, and brought his cheekbones into undue prominence. But he took his sheepskin with a bow and a gesture that extinguished several of his companions; and he faced the audience, on descending from the stage, with a composed effect gained by experience in the choir. The lustre in the ceiling lit up his yellow hair and his blue eyes: тАЬHe is as charming as ever!тАЭ thought Medora Phillips.

тАЬHeтАЩs had a hard pull of it,тАЭ commented Randolph.

тАЬI hope his own people will feed him up this summer,тАЭ said Medora. Her emphasis was wayward; тАЬHe wouldnтАЩt let me do it,тАЭ she seemed to mean.

тАЬNor me,тАЭ she almost made Randolph say.

There was a recessional, and then the crowds of students flooded the corridors and circulated under the fresh foliage of the campus. Randolph and Medora Phillips passed out with the rest of the assemblage. In the midst of one of the avenues of elms they noticed Cope as the center of a little group: two plain, elderly people (his parents, doubtless) andтБатАФandтБатАФ

Medora Phillips looked twice. Yes, the other figure was Carolyn Thorpe, offering congratulations. Carolyn had returned to her post and her work the day before. тАЬHтАЩm,тАЭ thought Medora, disposed to be miffed. Still, Carolyn had, after all, the same right to attend as anyone else.

Medora and Basil Randolph added their congratulations to CarolynтАЩs. Cope, still in academic garb, performed the necessary introductions. His air was eager, but cursory; smiling and ready, yet impersonal and cool; above all, expeditious. If his parents passed on with the impression that Medora Phillips and Basil Randolph were but casual acquaintances, worthy of nothing beyond brief formalities, the blame was his own.

тАЬIтАЩm showing father and mother over the campus,тАЭ he said, with an open smile and a wave with his diploma, as he edged away.

The elders docilely took their cue, and moved away with him.

тАЬWell,тАЭ said Randolph, тАЬthere are buildings, of course; and fountains, and sundials, and memorial benches; butтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬThey add nothing to him,тАЭ pronounced Medora, as she looked back on the retiring party.

тАЬDid you expect them to?тАЭ he asked. тАЬCharm, like guilt, is personal. Anyhow, there seems to be no brother,тАЭ he added.

тАЬWell, come, Carolyn,тАЭ said Medora, to her returned secretary, who was looking after the party too; тАЬletтАЩs start for home. Good afternoon, Basil.тАЭ

тАЬWhat nice, good, pleasant, friendly people they are!тАЭ breathed Carolyn.

Randolph had strolled away, and Medora Phillips turned a studious glance on her companion. Carolyn was conceivably in a state of mindтБатАФkeyed up to an all-inclusive appreciation. Did that foreshadow further verse?тБатАФa rustic rhapsody, a provincial pantoum? But Medora withheld question. Much as she would have enjoyed a well-consolidated impression of the visitors, she did not intend to secure it by interrogating Carolyn Thorpe.
